enrichment.short_description: Boopos was a fintech lender that provided acquisition financing for buying online businesses such as e-commerce stores, Amazon FBA brands, and SaaS companies; it was acquired by Founderpath in 2025 and no longer accepts new loan applications directly.
enrichment.starting_price: Historical acquisition loan rates of 17% to 23% APR; new applications now route through Founderpath
enrichment.headquarters: Miami, Florida, United States (with a Madrid, Spain office)
enrichment.seo_title: Boopos: Acquisition Financing for Online Businesses
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Boopos provided loans to acquire e-commerce and SaaS businesses. Now part of Founderpath. See how its financing, rates, and terms worked.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Boopos was a fintech company founded in 2020, headquartered in Miami, Florida with an additional office in Madrid, Spain, that specialized in acquisition financing for buyers of online businesses, including e-commerce stores, Amazon FBA brands, SaaS companies, and other subscription or content-based digital businesses. Its core product was a revenue-based acquisition loan that could fund up to 80 percent of a purchase price, structured as non-dilutive debt that did not require personal guarantees from the borrower, distinguishing it from traditional bank loans or equity investment. Boopos advertised a fast underwriting process, claiming it could evaluate an online business and issue a term sheet within 2 to 3 business days, with funding available within 7 days of an executed letter of intent. Historical interest rates on Boopos loans ranged from approximately 17 to 23 percent APR. The company also offered adjacent services such as business valuations and advisory calls to support buyers navigating acquisitions. Boopos raised a total of roughly 263 million dollars across debt facilities and equity, including a 30 million dollar Series Seed round in February 2022 led by Fasanara and a 58 million dollar Series A round led by Bonsai Partners later that year. In March 2025, Boopos was acquired by Founderpath, a financing platform for SaaS founders, in an eight-figure deal. As a direct result of that acquisition, Boopos stopped accepting new loan applications; its website now directs prospective borrowers to explore financing options through Founderpath instead. Existing Boopos borrowers at the time of the acquisition continued to have their loans serviced under original terms, with no immediate changes to payments or support. Because Boopos no longer originates new acquisition loans as a standalone brand, it is best understood today as a discontinued direct-lending product whose technology, underwriting expertise, and loan book were absorbed into Founderpath's broader SaaS financing offering.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Boopos
paragraphs: Boopos was a fintech platform built specifically to finance the acquisition of online businesses, a niche that traditional banks historically underserved because they lacked the tools to evaluate digital cash flows such as Amazon FBA revenue, SaaS monthly recurring revenue, or e-commerce store margins. Founded in 2020 and based in Miami with a Madrid engineering presence, Boopos built underwriting models around the specific financial signals of online businesses rather than the collateral-based models used by conventional commercial lenders.

The company positioned itself as an alternative to seller financing, personal savings, or dilutive equity investment for entrepreneurs looking to buy an existing online business rather than build one from scratch. By funding up to 80 percent of a purchase price with revenue-based repayment, Boopos aimed to let buyers close acquisitions faster and with less personal financial risk than a conventional Small Business Administration loan.

In March 2025, Boopos was acquired by Founderpath, a company focused on financing for SaaS founders. Following the acquisition, Boopos stopped accepting new loan applications, and its website now redirects prospective borrowers toward Founderpath's financing products. Anyone researching Boopos today should treat it as a discontinued standalone lender whose loan book and underwriting approach live on inside Founderpath.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Boopos' signature product was its acquisition loan, which could finance up to 80 percent of a purchase price for an online business and was repaid on a revenue-based schedule tied to the acquired business's performance rather than a fixed amortization schedule typical of bank debt.

The financing was structured as non-dilutive debt with no personal guarantees required from the borrower, meaning buyers did not have to pledge personal assets or give up equity in exchange for capital, which was a meaningful differentiator from both traditional bank loans and venture or private equity investment.

Boopos also emphasized underwriting speed, claiming it could evaluate an online business, issue a term sheet within 2 to 3 business days, and fund a deal within 7 days of an executed letter of intent, considerably faster than the multi-week or multi-month timelines common with SBA loans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Boopos did not use a subscription pricing model since it was a lender, not a software product; instead it charged interest on acquisition loans, with historical rates ranging from approximately 17 to 23 percent APR depending on the risk profile of the underlying business and the buyer.

Because Boopos no longer originates new loans following its 2025 acquisition by Founderpath, there is no current published pricing for new applicants; those seeking acquisition financing for an online business are directed to apply through Founderpath instead.

Existing Boopos borrowers whose loans originated before the acquisition continued to have their loans serviced under the original terms and rates that were in place at the time of closing, with no immediate changes to payments or support communicated at the time of the transition.

features.title: Rapid funding after LOI
features.description: Could fund a closed deal within 7 days of an executed letter of intent.

features.title: Business valuation tools
features.description: Offered valuation resources to help buyers assess a target online business before making an offer.

features.title: Advisory calls
features.description: Provided access to advisory conversations to support buyers through the acquisition process.

features.description: Specialized underwriting covered Amazon FBA brands, e-commerce stores, SaaS companies, and content sites.

Financing was narrowly focused on online and digital businesses, not general small business acquisitions
Prospective borrowers must now go through Founderpath rather than Boopos directly

faqs.answer: Boopos could fund up to 80 percent of an online business acquisition price under its revenue-based loan structure.

faqs.question: Did Boopos require personal guarantees?
faqs.answer: No, Boopos loans were structured as non-dilutive debt without personal guarantee requirements.

faqs.question: What happens to existing Boopos borrowers?
faqs.answer: Existing borrowers continue to have their loans serviced under the original terms, with no immediate change to payments or support following the Founderpath acquisition.

faqs.question: How fast could Boopos fund a deal?
faqs.answer: Boopos advertised term sheets within 2 to 3 business days and funding within 7 days of an executed letter of intent.

faqs.question: How much funding did Boopos raise?
faqs.answer: Boopos raised approximately 263 million dollars in total, including a 30 million dollar Series Seed round in 2022 and a 58 million dollar Series A round the same year.

enrichment.short_description: BoostUp is an AI-powered revenue intelligence and forecasting platform that helps B2B sales teams unify pipeline analytics, deal inspection, and conversation intelligence to predict revenue outcomes more accurately.
enrichment.starting_price: From approximately 79 dollars per user per month, with final pricing custom-quoted based on users and modules
enrichment.seo_title: BoostUp: AI Revenue Intelligence and Forecasting
enrichment.seo_meta_description: BoostUp is an AI revenue intelligence platform for B2B sales teams, unifying forecasting, pipeline analytics, and deal inspection in one place.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: BoostUp, operating as BoostUp.ai, is an AI-powered revenue operations and intelligence platform founded in 2018 and headquartered in Santa Clara, California, with roughly 51 to 200 employees. The platform is built for B2B revenue teams, particularly mid-market and enterprise sales organizations, that need highly accurate, multi-dimensional forecasting across complex revenue models such as SaaS subscriptions, consumption-based pricing, product-led growth motions, and renewals. BoostUp's core product, marketed as its Revenue Command Center, unifies several functions that are traditionally handled by separate point solutions: AI-driven machine forecasting that models pipeline data to predict close rates and revenue outcomes, advanced pipeline analytics and waterfall reporting that track how deals move and slip across a quarter, deal and engagement risk scoring that flags at-risk opportunities before they are lost, and AI-guided deal inspection that surfaces coaching insights for sales managers during pipeline reviews. The platform also includes RevBI, a self-service revenue business intelligence layer that lets revenue operations teams build custom reports and dashboards without relying on data teams, native conversation intelligence that analyzes sales calls, and BoostBot, an AI agent layer that generates alerts and next-best-action recommendations for reps and managers. Automatic activity capture pulls data from email, calendar, and conferencing tools to keep CRM records current without manual entry, and mobile apps let reps and managers update deals and submit forecasts away from their desks. BoostUp positions itself as a competitor to other revenue intelligence platforms such as Clari and Gong's forecasting tools, differentiating on its emphasis on multi-dimensional, model-driven forecasting accuracy for complex go-to-market motions rather than a single linear sales pipeline. Pricing is not fully transparent; third-party sources report a published starting price near 79 dollars per user per month with a free trial, but actual cost depends on the number of users, the specific modules deployed such as forecasting versus RevBI, and the size of the deployment, meaning most customers receive a custom quote through BoostUp's sales team rather than self-service checkout.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is BoostUp
paragraphs: BoostUp is a revenue intelligence platform designed to help B2B sales and revenue operations teams forecast more accurately and manage pipeline health with less manual spreadsheet work. Founded in 2018 and based in Santa Clara, California, the company built its platform around the idea that traditional CRM pipeline views are insufficient for predicting revenue in complex go-to-market models involving subscriptions, usage-based pricing, and renewals.

The platform, marketed as the Revenue Command Center, combines AI-driven forecasting, pipeline analytics, deal risk scoring, and conversation intelligence into a single system that sits on top of a company's existing CRM, typically Salesforce, rather than replacing it. This lets revenue leaders get a consolidated view of forecast accuracy, deal health, and rep activity without switching between multiple tools.

BoostUp is aimed primarily at mid-market and enterprise B2B revenue organizations with dedicated sales operations or revenue operations functions, since the platform's value grows with the complexity of the sales motion it is modeling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the center of BoostUp's platform is AI-powered machine forecasting, which analyzes historical pipeline patterns to generate predictive revenue forecasts alongside the traditional manual, rep-submitted forecast, helping leadership spot discrepancies between what reps believe will close and what the data suggests.

Pipeline analytics and waterfall reporting give revenue operations teams a way to visualize how pipeline value changes over the course of a quarter, including slippage, new pipeline creation, and closed-won or closed-lost movement, which is central to diagnosing forecast misses.

Deal and engagement risk scoring, combined with AI-guided deal inspection, flags opportunities that show warning signs such as stalled activity or missing stakeholders, while RevBI gives operations teams a self-service way to build custom revenue reports without engineering support. Native conversation intelligence and the BoostBot AI agent layer round out the platform by surfacing next-best-action alerts drawn from calls, emails, and calendar activity.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: BoostUp does not publish a fully transparent pricing table on its website; access typically requires a demo request and a custom quote from its sales team, which is common for enterprise revenue intelligence software.

Third-party software marketplaces report a starting price around 79 dollars per user per month, with a free trial available, though this figure should be treated as an indicative starting point rather than a fixed rate.

Final pricing depends on the number of licensed users, which specific modules are deployed such as forecasting, RevBI, or conversation intelligence, and the overall size and complexity of the deployment, so prospective buyers should expect a sales-led quoting process rather than self-service signup.

faqs.answer: BoostUp is used by B2B revenue teams for AI-powered sales forecasting, pipeline analytics, deal risk scoring, and revenue business intelligence.

faqs.question: How much does BoostUp cost?
faqs.answer: BoostUp does not publish full pricing; third-party sources cite a starting point near 79 dollars per user per month, but most customers receive a custom quote based on users and modules.

faqs.question: Does BoostUp replace Salesforce or a CRM?
faqs.answer: No, BoostUp typically sits on top of an existing CRM such as Salesforce, layering forecasting and analytics on top of CRM data rather than replacing it.

faqs.question: Who is BoostUp built for?
faqs.answer: BoostUp is built primarily for mid-market and enterprise B2B revenue teams with dedicated sales or revenue operations functions.

faqs.answer: It is BoostUp's unified platform branding for its combined forecasting, pipeline analytics, deal inspection, and conversation intelligence capabilities.

faqs.answer: Yes, BoostUp includes native conversation intelligence for analyzing sales calls alongside its forecasting and pipeline tools.

faqs.question: When was BoostUp founded?
faqs.answer: BoostUp was founded in 2018 and is headquartered in Santa Clara, California.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial for BoostUp?
faqs.answer: Some third-party listings indicate a free trial is available, though prospective customers should confirm current trial availability directly with BoostUp.

enrichment.short_description: BootstrapCDN is a free content delivery network that hosts the Bootstrap CSS and JavaScript framework along with Font Awesome icons, now operated and maintained by jsDelivr.
enrichment.seo_title: BootstrapCDN: Free CDN for Bootstrap and Font Awesome
enrichment.seo_meta_description: BootstrapCDN is a free CDN hosting the Bootstrap framework and Font Awesome icons, now powered and maintained by jsDelivr infrastructure.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: BootstrapCDN is a free content delivery network dedicated to hosting the Bootstrap CSS and JavaScript framework, along with the Font Awesome icon library, so developers can include them in web projects via a hosted link instead of downloading and self-hosting the files. It was launched in 2012 by David Henzel and Justin Dorfman while working at MaxCDN, and for years operated as MaxCDN's flagship open-source project, serving Bootstrap assets to a large share of the web's front-end developers. Around 2021, BootstrapCDN transitioned to be operated and maintained by jsDelivr, a separate open-source CDN project; jsDelivr took over full ownership of the bootstrapcdn.com domain and folded BootstrapCDN's infrastructure into its own network. As part of that transition, legacy hostnames that developers had embedded in millions of existing websites, including stackpath.bootstrapcdn.com and maxcdn.bootstrapcdn.com, were migrated to new backing infrastructure without requiring any action from site owners, preserving backward compatibility for old code snippets. Today the service's traffic and DNS are handled by Cloudflare, origin storage sits on Amazon S3, and BunnyCDN provides origin shielding along with URL rewriting and HTTP header handling. jsDelivr states it will continue to support all previously issued BootstrapCDN hostnames and URLs indefinitely, meaning old integration snippets referencing bootstrapcdn.com or its legacy subdomains keep working. BootstrapCDN itself charges nothing to use; it is funded and maintained as an open infrastructure project rather than a commercial product, and is largely maintained by a small team associated with jsDelivr, including maintainer Dmitriy Akulov. The core use case for BootstrapCDN has not changed since 2012: developers add a script or link tag pointing at BootstrapCDN's hosted files to get Bootstrap's CSS grid, components, and JavaScript plugins, or Font Awesome's icon set, without managing local copies, benefiting from CDN caching, browser cache sharing across sites that use the same public CDN URL, and geographically distributed edge servers for faster load times.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is BootstrapCDN
paragraphs: BootstrapCDN is a free, publicly hosted content delivery network that serves the Bootstrap front-end framework's CSS and JavaScript files, along with the Font Awesome icon library, so that developers do not need to download, host, or update these assets themselves. It was launched in 2012 by David Henzel and Justin Dorfman while they were at MaxCDN, and quickly became one of the most widely referenced public CDNs on the web due to Bootstrap's popularity.

The service works on a simple model: developers copy a link or script tag pointing to a BootstrapCDN-hosted file into their HTML, and the browser fetches that file from the nearest edge server rather than from the developer's own hosting infrastructure. Because so many sites reference the same public URLs, browsers can also benefit from shared caching across different websites using the identical CDN link.

In a significant operational change, BootstrapCDN moved under the stewardship of jsDelivr around 2021. jsDelivr assumed full ownership of the bootstrapcdn.com domain and migrated the underlying delivery infrastructure while preserving every previously issued hostname, so legacy code referencing old MaxCDN or StackPath-branded BootstrapCDN URLs continues to function without any changes required from site owners.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core feature of BootstrapCDN is free, always-current hosting of the Bootstrap framework's compiled CSS and JavaScript bundles across multiple Bootstrap versions, letting developers pin to a specific release or track the latest stable build via a hosted URL.

It also hosts Font Awesome, the widely used icon library, meaning a single service covers two of the most common front-end dependencies used in traditional multi-page websites and legacy jQuery-based projects.

Under jsDelivr's operation, the service benefits from a modern CDN stack: Cloudflare handles DNS and edge traffic routing, origin files are stored in Amazon S3, and BunnyCDN provides origin shielding, URL rewriting, and HTTP header management, giving the free service enterprise-grade reliability and global edge coverage.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: BootstrapCDN is entirely free to use for any website or project; there is no paid tier, subscription, or usage-based billing for developers linking to its hosted Bootstrap or Font Awesome files.

The service is funded as open infrastructure rather than a commercial product, similar to how jsDelivr funds its broader open-source CDN network through sponsorships and its own backing infrastructure providers rather than charging end users.
Because there is no account system or paid plan, there is nothing to sign up for or subscribe to; developers simply reference the public CDN URLs directly in their project's markup.

features.title: Global edge network
features.description: Uses Cloudflare for DNS and edge routing to serve files from locations close to end users.

features.title: Origin shielding via BunnyCDN
features.description: BunnyCDN protects origin storage and handles URL rewrites and HTTP header changes.

features.title: S3-backed origin storage
features.description: Origin files are stored on Amazon S3 for durability and reliability.

features.title: No account required
features.description: Developers can use the CDN links directly without registering for an account.

features.title: Version pinning
features.description: Supports linking to specific Bootstrap and Font Awesome version numbers for stability.

pros: Completely free with no usage limits for typical website integration
Backed by jsDelivr's modern, reliable CDN infrastructure
Preserves backward compatibility with years of legacy code referencing old URLs
Widely recognized and trusted CDN used across millions of websites
cons: No official support channel comparable to a paid commercial CDN
Relies on third-party public CDN links, which some security-conscious teams avoid in favor of self-hosting
Less relevant for modern build-tool-based projects that install Bootstrap via a package manager
No service-level agreement typical of paid enterprise CDN products

faqs.question: Is BootstrapCDN still active in 2026?
faqs.answer: Yes, BootstrapCDN is active and operated by jsDelivr, which maintains the domain and all legacy hostnames.

faqs.question: Who operates BootstrapCDN now?
faqs.answer: BootstrapCDN is operated and maintained by jsDelivr, which took over full ownership of the bootstrapcdn.com domain around 2021.

faqs.question: Is BootstrapCDN free?
faqs.answer: Yes, BootstrapCDN is completely free to use for any website or project.

faqs.question: Does BootstrapCDN host Font Awesome too?
faqs.answer: Yes, BootstrapCDN hosts both the Bootstrap framework and the Font Awesome icon library.

faqs.question: Will old BootstrapCDN links from MaxCDN or StackPath still work?
faqs.answer: Yes, jsDelivr has committed to continuing support for all previously issued BootstrapCDN hostnames and URLs indefinitely.

faqs.question: Who originally created BootstrapCDN?
faqs.answer: BootstrapCDN was launched in 2012 by David Henzel and Justin Dorfman while at MaxCDN.

faqs.question: What infrastructure powers BootstrapCDN today?
faqs.answer: Cloudflare handles DNS and edge traffic, origin files sit on Amazon S3, and BunnyCDN provides origin shielding and header handling.

faqs.question: Do I need an account to use BootstrapCDN?
faqs.answer: No, developers can use the public CDN links directly in their project without creating an account.

enrichment.short_description: BorgBase is a backup hosting service purpose-built for BorgBackup and Restic, the open-source deduplicating backup tools, offering encrypted repository storage for developers and technical users.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (10 GB, 2 repositories); paid plans from 24 dollars per year for 250 GB
enrichment.company_size: Small team, operated by Peakford Ltd
enrichment.seo_title: BorgBase: Hosting for BorgBackup and Restic Repositories
enrichment.seo_meta_description: BorgBase offers secure, encrypted hosting for BorgBackup and Restic repositories, with a free 10 GB tier and paid plans from 24 dollars a year.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: BorgBase is a backup hosting service built specifically around BorgBackup and Restic, two popular open-source deduplicating backup tools favored by developers, sysadmins, and technically inclined home users. Rather than being a general-purpose cloud storage provider, BorgBase provides remote repository hosting purpose-built for how Borg and Restic work, including support for append-only mode that protects backups from being altered or deleted even if a client machine is compromised by ransomware, end-to-end encryption where BorgBase itself cannot read the backed-up data, two-factor authentication for account security, and monitoring and alerting via email, Pushover, or webhook so users know if a scheduled backup fails to run. The service is operated by Peakford Ltd, a small company based in Naxxar, Malta, that also operates PikaPods, a managed hosting service for self-hosted open-source applications; the team describes itself as having more than a decade of experience running boutique web and backup services. BorgBase offers a permanently free tier with 10 GB of storage and 2 repositories, making it accessible for individuals to try before committing to a paid plan. Paid plans are tiered by storage: a Small plan offers 250 GB for 24 dollars per year, a Medium plan offers 1 TB for 90 dollars per year, and a Large plan offers 2 TB for 165 dollars per year or 17 dollars per month, with all paid tiers including unlimited or near-unlimited repositories depending on the tier and the choice of EU or US hosting regions. Beyond the base storage included in a plan, BorgBase allows flexible overage usage up to roughly three times the plan's base storage, billed only for what is actually used, and the company does not charge separately for uploads, downloads, or API calls, which distinguishes it from many general-purpose cloud storage providers that charge for egress. BorgBase also offers a 30-day refund window on annual plans and provides API access for automating repository management. Because it is narrowly focused on the Borg and Restic ecosystem, BorgBase is primarily used by developers, sysadmins, and privacy-conscious individuals who already use Borg or Restic locally and need a reliable, encrypted, off-site destination for their backup repositories rather than a general file-sync or cloud-drive product.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is BorgBase
paragraphs: BorgBase is a specialized hosting service for BorgBackup and Restic, two open-source, deduplicating backup tools that are popular among developers and system administrators for their efficiency and strong encryption support. Instead of building a general cloud storage product, BorgBase focuses exclusively on being a reliable, secure remote destination for Borg and Restic repositories.

The service is operated by Peakford Ltd, a small company based in Naxxar, Malta, which also runs PikaPods, a managed hosting platform for self-hosted open-source applications. The team positions itself as deeply embedded in the open-source backup community, sponsoring Borg's development and staying active in related forums.

BorgBase's core value proposition is that it understands the operational needs of Borg and Restic users specifically, offering features like append-only repositories that protect against ransomware tampering, which general-purpose cloud storage services typically do not offer out of the box.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Append-only mode is one of BorgBase's most important security features: it lets a repository accept new backup data while preventing existing archives from being deleted or modified, which protects backups even if the client machine running Borg is later compromised by ransomware or a malicious actor.

End-to-end encryption means all backup data is encrypted client-side before it ever reaches BorgBase's servers, so the company itself cannot read the contents of user backups, an important property for privacy-conscious users and organizations handling sensitive data.

Operational features round out the service: two-factor authentication protects account access, monitoring and alerting via email, Pushover, or webhook notifies users if a scheduled backup job fails to check in, and an API allows automation of repository creation and management for infrastructure-as-code workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: BorgBase offers a free tier that includes 10 GB of storage and 2 repositories forever, with no time limit, making it a low-risk way to test the service with a real backup workflow before paying.

Paid plans are priced by base storage: the Small plan includes 250 GB for 24 dollars per year, the Medium plan includes 1 TB for 90 dollars per year, and the Large plan includes 2 TB for 165 dollars per year or 17 dollars per month, with all plans available in either EU or US hosting regions.

Users can exceed their plan's base storage using flexible overage of up to roughly three times the included amount, paying only for the extra space actually used, and BorgBase does not charge for uploads, downloads, or API calls. Annual plans include a 30-day refund window.

features.title: Append-only repository mode
features.description: Protects existing backup archives from deletion or modification even if the client is compromised.

features.title: End-to-end encryption
features.description: Backup data is encrypted client-side before reaching BorgBase servers, so the provider cannot read it.

features.title: Two-factor authentication
features.description: Secures account access with an additional verification step.

features.title: Monitoring and alerts
features.description: Notifies users via email, Pushover, or webhook if a scheduled backup fails to run.

features.title: Flexible storage overage
features.description: Allows usage beyond the base plan, up to about three times the included storage, billed only for what is used.

features.title: EU and US hosting regions
features.description: Lets users choose where their backup repository is physically hosted.

features.title: API access
features.description: Enables automation of repository creation and management.

features.title: Free forever tier
features.description: Provides 10 GB of storage and 2 repositories at no cost with no time limit.

pros: Purpose-built for BorgBackup and Restic rather than a generic storage product
No charges for uploads, downloads, or API calls unlike many cloud storage providers
Append-only mode offers strong protection against ransomware tampering
Permanently free tier makes it easy to test before committing to a paid plan
cons: Narrow focus means it is not useful as general-purpose cloud storage
Small company size means less brand recognition than major cloud providers
Only two hosting regions, EU and US, limits geographic choice for latency-sensitive use cases
Requires familiarity with Borg or Restic command-line tools to get full value

faqs.question: What is BorgBase used for?
faqs.answer: BorgBase is used to host encrypted, off-site backup repositories for the BorgBackup and Restic open-source backup tools.

faqs.question: Does BorgBase have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, BorgBase offers 10 GB of storage and 2 repositories free forever with no time limit.

faqs.question: Can BorgBase read my backup data?
faqs.answer: No, backups are end-to-end encrypted client-side before upload, so BorgBase cannot read the contents.

faqs.question: Does BorgBase support Restic as well as Borg?
faqs.answer: Yes, BorgBase hosts repositories for both BorgBackup and Restic.

faqs.question: Where is BorgBase based?
faqs.answer: BorgBase is operated by Peakford Ltd, a company based in Naxxar, Malta.

faqs.question: What is append-only mode?
faqs.answer: It is a repository setting that allows new backups to be written while preventing existing archives from being deleted or altered, protecting against ransomware.

faqs.question: Does BorgBase charge for bandwidth?
faqs.answer: No, BorgBase does not charge separately for uploads, downloads, or API calls beyond the storage plan cost.

faqs.question: What hosting regions does BorgBase offer?
faqs.answer: BorgBase offers both EU and US hosting regions for repositories.

use_cases.title: Off-site backup for developer workstations
use_cases.description: A developer configures Borg to automatically back up their laptop to a BorgBase repository nightly, protected by append-only mode.

use_cases.title: Server backup for sysadmins
use_cases.description: A sysadmin uses Restic with BorgBase as the remote target for encrypted backups of production servers, with monitoring alerts on failure.

use_cases.title: Ransomware-resistant backup strategy
use_cases.description: A small business uses BorgBase's append-only repositories as an off-site layer of its backup strategy that cannot be altered even if local systems are compromised.

pricing_plans.price: 165 dollars per year or 17 dollars per month

enrichment.short_description: Bottles is a free, open-source Linux application that provides a graphical interface for creating and managing isolated Wine environments, making it easier to run Windows software and games on Linux.
enrichment.seo_title: Bottles: Run Windows Apps and Games on Linux
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bottles is a free, open-source tool that lets you run Windows software and games on Linux using isolated, sandboxed Wine environments.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Bottles is a free and open-source Linux application, first made public around 2020, that provides a graphical, user-friendly interface for creating and managing isolated Wine prefixes, commonly called bottles, so that Windows software and games can run on Linux without requiring users to master Wine's command-line configuration directly. The project is maintained by the bottlesdevs organization on GitHub, with core contributors including Mirko Brombin, Francesco Masala, and Pietro Di Caprio; Brombin, an Italian UX designer who also created the VanillaOS Linux distribution, is closely associated with founding the project. Bottles is written in Python using the GTK toolkit and is licensed under the GPL-3.0 open-source license. Functionally, Bottles lets a user create a separate, isolated environment for each Windows application or game, automatically handling the download and installation of the appropriate Wine runner version and common Windows dependencies such as DirectX or .NET components, and organizing each environment so that different applications do not interfere with one another. A key architectural feature is sandboxing: Bottles is designed to confine each Windows application inside its environment, limiting what it can access on the host Linux system, which the project frames as both an organizational convenience and a security benefit that can help contain malware or misbehaving Windows software. The developers strongly recommend installing Bottles via Flatpak, distributed through Flathub under the package ID com.usebottles.bottles, because the Flatpak build is the only distribution method that provides Bottles' full sandboxing capability; other packaging formats exist but do not offer the same isolation guarantees. Bottles is commonly used by Linux gamers who want to run Windows-only games that lack native Linux ports or a Proton-based Steam integration, as well as by users who need specific Windows productivity or creative applications that have no Linux equivalent. As a fully open-source project with no paid tiers, Bottles is free for anyone to download, use, and modify, and its source code is publicly available on GitHub for community contribution.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Bottles
paragraphs: Bottles is a free, open-source Linux application that gives users a graphical way to create and manage isolated Wine environments, referred to as bottles, for running Windows software and games. Rather than replacing Wine, Bottles wraps it in an approachable interface so users do not need to memorize Wine's configuration flags and environment variables to get a Windows application running.

The project became publicly known around 2020 and is maintained by the bottlesdevs organization, with contributors including Mirko Brombin, Francesco Masala, and Pietro Di Caprio. It is written in Python with the GTK toolkit and released under the GPL-3.0 open-source license, meaning the source code is freely available for anyone to inspect, use, or contribute to.

Each bottle created by the application acts as its own self-contained Windows-like environment, letting users run multiple, unrelated Windows programs on the same Linux machine without one program's dependencies conflicting with another's, a common problem when managing Wine manually.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Bottles automatically manages Wine runner versions and common Windows dependencies such as DirectX and .NET components, installing what a given application needs inside its own bottle without requiring the user to hunt down and configure these components manually.

Sandboxing is a central design principle: Bottles aims to confine each Windows application's access to the host system, which both keeps environments organized by use case and helps limit the potential damage from malware or misbehaving software running inside a bottle.

The developers strongly recommend installing Bottles through Flatpak, distributed via Flathub, because the Flatpak build is the only packaging method that delivers Bottles' full sandboxing capability; this makes Flatpak the officially preferred installation path over other Linux packaging formats.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Bottles is completely free to download and use, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or premium features gated behind payment, consistent with its status as an open-source community project.

Because the source code is published under the GPL-3.0 license on GitHub, anyone is also free to inspect, modify, or redistribute the application, and there is no commercial entity charging for access to the core software.

The project accepts community contributions and, like many open-source desktop applications, may be supported through voluntary donations or sponsorships to its maintainers, but no functionality within Bottles itself requires payment.

features.title: Graphical Wine prefix manager
features.description: Provides a user-friendly interface for creating and configuring Wine environments without command-line configuration.

features.title: Isolated per-application environments
features.description: Each bottle keeps a Windows application's dependencies separate from other bottles on the same machine.

features.title: Automatic runner and dependency handling
features.description: Downloads and installs appropriate Wine runner versions and common Windows dependencies automatically.

features.title: Sandboxing for security
features.description: Confines each Windows application's system access, helping contain malware or misbehaving software.

features.title: Flatpak-first distribution
features.description: Officially recommended installation via Flathub, the only method offering full sandboxing capability.

features.title: Application categorization
features.description: Lets users organize bottles by the type of software or game they contain.

features.title: Open-source and free
features.description: Fully free to use, with source code published under the GPL-3.0 license on GitHub.

features.title: Cross-distribution support
features.description: Works across major Linux distributions that support Flatpak or the project's other packaging options.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tiers
Simplifies Wine configuration with a graphical, approachable interface
Sandboxing adds a security layer not present in raw Wine usage
Actively maintained by a dedicated open-source community
cons: Full sandboxing only available through the Flatpak installation method
Windows application compatibility still depends on Wine's underlying support for that software
Can require more disk space than a raw Wine setup due to isolated environments
Some advanced troubleshooting still requires familiarity with Wine concepts

faqs.question: Is Bottles free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Bottles is completely free and open-source software with no paid tiers.

faqs.question: What is a bottle in this context?
faqs.answer: A bottle is an isolated Wine environment created for running one or more Windows applications separately from other Windows software on the same Linux system.

faqs.question: How should I install Bottles?
faqs.answer: The developers strongly recommend installing Bottles via Flatpak through Flathub, since it is the only method that provides full sandboxing capability.

faqs.question: Does Bottles replace Wine?
faqs.answer: No, Bottles uses Wine as its underlying compatibility layer; it adds a graphical interface and management features on top of Wine rather than replacing it.

faqs.question: Can Bottles run Windows games?
faqs.answer: Yes, Bottles is commonly used to run Windows games on Linux, particularly titles without native Linux support.

faqs.question: What license is Bottles released under?
faqs.answer: Bottles is released under the GPL-3.0 open-source license.

faqs.question: Who maintains Bottles?
faqs.answer: Bottles is maintained by the bottlesdevs organization, with core contributors including Mirko Brombin, Francesco Masala, and Pietro Di Caprio.

faqs.question: Does Bottles protect against malware in Windows software?
faqs.answer: Bottles' sandboxing is designed to confine what a Windows application inside a bottle can access on the host system, which can help limit the impact of malware, though it is not a complete antivirus solution.

use_cases.title: Running Windows-only games on Linux
use_cases.description: A Linux gamer uses Bottles to create a dedicated environment for a Windows game that lacks a native Linux version or Proton support.

use_cases.title: Using Windows productivity software on Linux
use_cases.description: A user relies on Bottles to run a Windows-only creative or productivity application that has no adequate Linux alternative.
use_cases.audience: Linux desktop users switching from Windows

use_cases.title: Isolating legacy Windows software
use_cases.description: An IT hobbyist uses Bottles to sandbox an old Windows program in its own isolated environment to avoid dependency conflicts with other software.

enrichment.short_description: bottom, often invoked as btm, is a free, open-source, cross-platform terminal system resource monitor written in Rust that displays real-time CPU, memory, disk, network, and process information.
enrichment.company_size: Open-source project maintained by Clement Tsang
enrichment.seo_meta_description: bottom, run as btm, is a free open-source Rust terminal system monitor showing real-time CPU, memory, disk, and network graphs, cross-platform.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: bottom, distributed as the command-line binary btm, is a free and open-source, cross-platform system resource monitor for the terminal, written in the Rust programming language and maintained by developer Clement Tsang. It is designed as an alternative to classic Unix monitoring tools like top and htop, and its author cites gotop, gtop, and htop as direct inspirations for its design and feature set. bottom runs on Linux, macOS, and Windows, making it more broadly cross-platform than many terminal monitors that are Unix-only. The project is published under the MIT license and is available through Rust's package manager via cargo install bottom, as well as through numerous system package managers across the platforms it supports. Functionally, bottom presents a graphical, panel-based terminal interface showing real-time data: CPU usage per core and in aggregate, RAM and swap usage, network input and output throughput with support for zooming in and out on historical graphs, disk capacity and usage across mounted volumes, temperature sensor readings, and battery status on supported hardware. It also includes a searchable, sortable process list similar to htop, letting users filter, sort, and terminate processes directly from the terminal interface. A defining characteristic of bottom is its emphasis on customization: users can configure layouts, colors, and displayed widgets through a configuration file, tailoring which metrics appear and how they are arranged on screen. Because it is written in Rust, bottom is designed to have low memory overhead and high performance, aiming to run comfortably even on lower-specification machines where a heavier monitoring tool might itself consume noticeable resources. The project has attracted a substantial following in the developer and systems administration community, with its GitHub repository, maintained under the ClementTsang/bottom namespace, having accumulated more than 22,000 stars. As a fully open-source, MIT-licensed project, bottom has no pricing, subscription, or paid tier of any kind; it is entirely free to install and use.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is bottom
paragraphs: bottom is a free, open-source terminal-based system resource monitor written in Rust, run from the command line using the btm binary. It was created by developer Clement Tsang as a cross-platform, customizable alternative to classic tools like top and htop, drawing direct inspiration from gotop, gtop, and htop.

Unlike many terminal monitoring tools that only support Unix-like systems, bottom runs natively on Linux, macOS, and Windows, making it a rare option for teams or individuals who need one consistent monitoring tool across mixed operating system environments.

The project is published under the MIT license and is available through Rust's cargo package manager as well as through many operating system package managers, and it has grown into one of the more widely adopted Rust-based command-line monitoring tools, with its GitHub repository surpassing 22,000 stars.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: bottom presents real-time, graphical widgets directly in the terminal for CPU usage per core and in aggregate, RAM and swap memory usage, and network input and output throughput, with support for zooming in and out on historical graph data to spot trends over time.

It also displays disk capacity and usage across mounted volumes, temperature sensor readings, and battery status where supported by the underlying hardware, giving a fuller picture of system health than a basic process list alone.

A searchable and sortable process list, similar in spirit to htop, lets users filter running processes, sort by resource usage, and terminate processes directly from within bottom's terminal interface, all while the tool's layout, colors, and visible widgets remain configurable through a user-editable configuration file.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: bottom is completely free and open source, released under the MIT license, with no paid tier, subscription, or licensing fee of any kind for individual or commercial use.
Installation is free through multiple channels, including Rust's cargo install bottom command and a wide range of system package managers across Linux, macOS, and Windows.

As a community-maintained open-source project, bottom has no company behind it charging for access; its ongoing development is sustained by its maintainer and open-source contributors rather than commercial licensing revenue.

features.title: Real-time CPU graphs
features.description: Displays per-core and aggregate CPU usage with historical, zoomable graphs.

features.title: RAM and swap monitoring
features.description: Shows current memory and swap usage in real time within the terminal interface.

features.title: Network throughput graphs
features.description: Tracks network input and output over time with zoom support for historical data.

features.title: Disk usage widgets
features.description: Displays capacity and usage information across mounted disk volumes.

features.title: Temperature and battery sensors
features.description: Reports temperature sensor readings and battery status on supported hardware.

features.title: Searchable process list
features.description: Allows filtering, sorting, and terminating processes directly from the terminal.

features.title: Cross-platform support
features.description: Runs natively on Linux, macOS, and Windows from a single codebase.

features.title: Configurable layout and theming
features.description: Lets users customize widget layout, colors, and displayed metrics via a config file.

pros: Completely free and open source under the MIT license
Runs natively on Linux, macOS, and Windows unlike many Unix-only monitors
Low memory footprint due to being written in Rust
Highly customizable layout, widgets, and color themes
cons: Terminal-only interface with no graphical desktop application
Requires familiarity with command-line tools and configuration files for customization
Some advanced sensor features depend on hardware and OS support that can vary by system
No official commercial support channel since it is a community-maintained project

faqs.question: What is bottom (btm)?
faqs.answer: bottom is a free, open-source, cross-platform terminal system resource monitor written in Rust, run using the btm command.

faqs.question: Is bottom free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, bottom is fully free and open source under the MIT license with no paid tiers.

faqs.question: What platforms does bottom support?
faqs.answer: bottom runs natively on Linux, macOS, and Windows.

faqs.question: How do I install bottom?
faqs.answer: bottom can be installed via cargo install bottom or through many operating system package managers.

faqs.question: Who created bottom?
faqs.answer: bottom was created and is maintained by developer Clement Tsang.

faqs.question: What is bottom similar to?
faqs.answer: bottom is similar to htop, top, gotop, and gtop, offering a graphical terminal interface for system monitoring.

faqs.question: Can I customize bottom's appearance?
faqs.answer: Yes, bottom supports configuration files that let users customize layout, colors, and which widgets are displayed.

faqs.question: Does bottom show battery and temperature information?
faqs.answer: Yes, bottom can display temperature sensor readings and battery status where supported by the hardware.

use_cases.title: Server resource monitoring
use_cases.description: A sysadmin runs btm over SSH on a remote Linux server to quickly check CPU, memory, and disk health during an incident.

use_cases.title: Cross-platform development monitoring
use_cases.description: A developer working across Linux, macOS, and Windows machines uses bottom as a consistent monitoring tool on all three.

use_cases.title: Lightweight monitoring on low-spec machines
use_cases.description: A user on an older or resource-constrained machine uses bottom instead of a heavier GUI monitor to check system load without adding overhead.

enrichment.short_description: Boulevard is a business management and booking platform built specifically for salons, spas, and med spas, covering appointment scheduling, point of sale, payments, and client marketing.
enrichment.starting_price: 159 dollars per month per location (Essentials plan, billed annually)
enrichment.seo_title: Boulevard: Booking and Management Software for Salons
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Boulevard is booking and business management software for salons, spas, and med spas, covering scheduling, payments, POS, and marketing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Boulevard, marketed at joinblvd.com, is a business management and booking platform built specifically for appointment-based self-care businesses including hair salons, spas, and medical spas. It was founded in 2016 in Los Angeles by Matt Danna, who serves as CEO, and Sean Stavropoulos, the CTO, both former coworkers at Fullscreen, and the company remains headquartered in Los Angeles. Boulevard began as an online booking platform and has expanded into a full AI-driven software-as-a-service suite covering appointment scheduling, point-of-sale checkout, integrated payment processing, personalized text and email marketing, client relationship management, and reporting, aimed at helping self-care businesses run both front-desk operations and client retention marketing from one system. The company has raised approximately 188 million dollars in total funding since its founding, including a 70 million dollar Series C round, and most recently an 80 million dollar Series D round completed in July 2025 that valued the company at roughly 800 million dollars post-money, with investors including Index Ventures, VMG Partners, BoxGroup, Bonfire Ventures, and Ludlow Ventures; Boulevard reported roughly a 500 percent increase in revenue over the three years leading up to that raise. Boulevard's pricing is tiered by business type and location. For standard salons and spas, the Essentials plan costs 176 dollars per month per location or 159 dollars per month if billed annually, with 100 free two-way text messages and 500 free email blasts included per month; the Premier plan costs 293 dollars per month or 263 dollars per month annually with 250 free texts; and the Prestige plan costs 410 dollars per month or 369 dollars per month annually with 2,500 free texts. For medical spas, Boulevard offers separate Aesthetics-branded bundles: an Aesthetics Starter Bundle at 410 dollars per month, or 369 dollars annually, and an Aesthetics Bundle at 468 dollars per month, or 421 dollars annually. Boulevard also processes payments directly through its platform, charging 2.65 percent plus 15 cents per card-present transaction and 3.65 percent plus 15 cents per card-not-present transaction, and sells its own Boulevard Duo card reader hardware for 149 dollars. There is no free plan or free trial publicly listed; prospective customers typically go through a sales demo before subscribing. Boulevard also offers a dedicated product for multi-location salon and spa franchises, reflecting its focus on scaling operations across many locations rather than only single-location shops.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Boulevard
paragraphs: Boulevard is a business management and booking platform purpose-built for salons, spas, and medical spas, unifying the operational tools these appointment-based businesses need into a single system rather than requiring separate booking, payment, and marketing tools.

Founded in 2016 in Los Angeles by Matt Danna and Sean Stavropoulos, both former Fullscreen colleagues, Boulevard began as an online booking tool and has since grown into a broader, AI-driven SaaS platform covering scheduling, point of sale, payments, and client marketing for self-care businesses.

The company has raised roughly 188 million dollars in total funding, most recently an 80 million dollar Series D round in July 2025 that valued Boulevard at approximately 800 million dollars, reflecting significant growth in a niche vertical software category focused specifically on appointment-based beauty and wellness businesses.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, Boulevard provides appointment scheduling built around the specific needs of salons, spas, and med spas, including service menus, staff and room scheduling, and client booking through a branded online booking page.

The platform includes integrated point-of-sale checkout and payment processing, letting businesses take card-present and card-not-present payments directly through Boulevard rather than a separate payment processor, along with proprietary card reader hardware.

Client marketing tools round out the platform, including two-way text messaging and email marketing campaigns for retention and rebooking, along with client relationship management and reporting features tailored to tracking business performance across one or multiple locations, with a dedicated offering for multi-location salon and spa franchises.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: For standard salons and spas, Boulevard offers three tiers per location: Essentials at 176 dollars per month, or 159 dollars per month billed annually, with 100 free two-way texts and 500 free email blasts monthly; Premier at 293 dollars per month, or 263 dollars annually, with 250 free texts; and Prestige at 410 dollars per month, or 369 dollars annually, with 2,500 free texts.

Medical spas are served by separate Aesthetics-branded bundles, with an Aesthetics Starter Bundle at 410 dollars per month, or 369 dollars annually, and a higher Aesthetics Bundle at 468 dollars per month, or 421 dollars annually.

Payment processing is billed separately from the subscription at 2.65 percent plus 15 cents per card-present transaction and 3.65 percent plus 15 cents per card-not-present transaction, and the optional Boulevard Duo card reader costs 149 dollars; there is no publicly listed free plan or free trial, so pricing typically requires going through a sales demo.

Combines scheduling, POS, payments, and marketing in a single platform
Strong funding and growth trajectory suggest ongoing product investment
Dedicated tools for multi-location and franchise businesses
cons: No publicly listed free plan or free trial
Pricing is higher than many general-purpose scheduling tools, especially at higher tiers
Payment processing fees apply on top of the monthly subscription
Per-location pricing can become expensive quickly for multi-location businesses

faqs.answer: Boulevard is built specifically for salons, spas, and medical spas, covering scheduling, point of sale, payments, and marketing.

faqs.answer: Standard plans range from 159 to 410 dollars per month per location depending on the tier and billing cycle, with separate Aesthetics bundles for med spas priced from 369 to 468 dollars per month.

faqs.answer: Boulevard does not publicly list a free plan or free trial; prospective customers typically go through a sales demo.

faqs.answer: Boulevard charges 2.65 percent plus 15 cents for card-present transactions and 3.65 percent plus 15 cents for card-not-present transactions.

faqs.answer: Boulevard was founded in 2016 by Matt Danna and Sean Stavropoulos, former coworkers at Fullscreen.

faqs.question: How much funding has Boulevard raised?

faqs.question: Does Boulevard support multiple locations or franchises?
faqs.answer: Yes, Boulevard offers dedicated pricing and features for salon and spa businesses operating across multiple locations or franchises.

faqs.question: Where is Boulevard headquartered?
faqs.answer: Boulevard is headquartered in Los Angeles, California.

enrichment.short_description: Box is a publicly traded (NYSE: BOX) enterprise cloud content management platform that lets organizations securely store, share, collaborate on, and govern files and business content, now increasingly built around AI-powered content workflows.
enrichment.starting_price: 5 dollars per user per month (Business Starter plan, billed annually, 3-user minimum)
enrichment.seo_title: Box: Enterprise Cloud Content Management Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Box (NYSE: BOX) is an enterprise cloud content management platform for secure file storage, sharing, and AI-powered content workflows.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Box, Inc. is a publicly traded enterprise cloud content management company, listed on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker BOX, founded in 2005 by Aaron Levie and Dylan Smith and headquartered in Redwood City, California. Levie remains CEO and Smith has served as CFO. Box went public via IPO in January 2015. The company describes its category as Intelligent Content Management, or ICM, positioning its platform beyond simple file storage into secure content collaboration, workflow automation, e-signature through Box Sign, security and compliance controls through Box Shield and Box Governance, and increasingly AI-powered features branded as Box AI that let organizations extract insights, summarize documents, and automate processes across stored content. For its fiscal year 2026, ending January 31, 2026, Box reported revenue of approximately 1.18 billion dollars, representing roughly 8 percent year-over-year growth. The company had approximately 2,912 employees as of the end of fiscal 2026, an increase of about 102 employees, or 3.63 percent, from the prior year. Box serves a large enterprise customer base, with over 100,000 businesses using the platform globally and roughly 68 percent of the Fortune 500 among its customers; it counts nearly 2,000 customers paying at least 100,000 dollars annually, up 8 percent year over year, and its newer Enterprise Advanced tier accounts for roughly 10 percent of total revenue, reflecting a push toward higher-value AI and governance features. Box's pricing is structured across five tiers: Business Starter at 5 dollars per user per month, Business at 15 dollars, Business Plus at 25 dollars, and Enterprise at 35 dollars per user per month, all billed annually with a three-user minimum, plus a custom-quoted Enterprise Plus tier for the largest organizations; monthly billing carries a roughly 25 to 40 percent premium over annual pricing across these tiers. A free Individual plan with limited storage is also available for personal use. Box integrates with major productivity and business software including Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, Salesforce, and Slack, and offers additional paid add-ons such as Box Shield for threat detection, Box Governance for retention and legal hold, and Box KeySafe for customer-managed encryption keys. Competitors in Box's market include Dropbox, Microsoft OneDrive and SharePoint, and Google Drive, though Box differentiates itself by focusing specifically on enterprise content governance, security, and workflow needs rather than general consumer file sync.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Box
paragraphs: Box is a publicly traded enterprise cloud content management company, trading on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker BOX. It was founded in 2005 by Aaron Levie, who remains CEO, and Dylan Smith, who has served as CFO, and the company is headquartered in Redwood City, California. Box completed its initial public offering in January 2015.

Box began as a cloud file storage and sharing service positioned against consumer-oriented competitors, but has since repositioned itself around what it calls Intelligent Content Management, or ICM, emphasizing secure enterprise content collaboration, workflow automation, governance, and increasingly AI-powered document intelligence rather than simple file sync.

As of fiscal year 2026, which ended January 31, 2026, Box reported revenue of approximately 1.18 billion dollars, up roughly 8 percent year over year, with approximately 2,912 employees, an increase of about 102 from the prior fiscal year. The company serves over 100,000 businesses globally, including roughly 68 percent of the Fortune 500, and counts nearly 2,000 customers paying at least 100,000 dollars annually.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, Box provides secure cloud storage and file sharing with granular permission controls, letting organizations manage who can view, edit, download, or share specific files and folders, along with detailed audit trails for compliance purposes.

Box AI is the company's suite of AI-powered content features, enabling automated document summarization, question answering over stored content, data extraction from unstructured files, and AI-assisted classification, reflecting the company's strategic push into AI-driven content workflows as a growth driver.

Additional platform capabilities include Box Sign for native e-signature workflows, Box Shield for threat detection and malware protection, Box Governance for retention policies and legal hold, Box Relay for workflow automation, and Box KeySafe for customer-managed encryption keys, alongside deep integrations with Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, Salesforce, and Slack that let Box serve as a secure content layer underneath an organization's existing productivity tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Box offers a free Individual plan with limited storage intended for personal use, alongside four core paid business tiers billed annually with a three-user minimum: Business Starter at 5 dollars per user per month, Business at 15 dollars, Business Plus at 25 dollars, and Enterprise at 35 dollars per user per month.

A fifth tier, Enterprise Plus, is available for the largest organizations with custom, sales-negotiated pricing that is not publicly published, typically including additional security, governance, and support commitments beyond the standard Enterprise plan.

Monthly billing is available across the published tiers but carries a meaningful premium over annual pricing, and higher tiers unlock progressively larger file upload limits, more advanced AI and governance features, and options like dedicated infrastructure and custom app development support.

features.title: Secure cloud content storage
features.description: Stores and organizes business files with granular, role-based permission controls and detailed audit trails.

features.title: Box AI content intelligence
features.description: Provides AI-powered summarization, question answering, and data extraction across stored documents.

features.title: Box Sign e-signature
features.description: Enables native electronic signature workflows without a separate third-party e-signature tool.

features.title: Box Shield security
features.description: Adds threat detection, malware protection, and smart access controls for sensitive content.

features.title: Box Governance
features.description: Manages data retention policies, legal holds, and compliance requirements for enterprise content.

features.title: Box Relay workflow automation
features.description: Automates multi-step business processes involving document review and approval.

features.title: Deep platform integrations
features.description: Connects natively with Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, Salesforce, and Slack among other enterprise tools.

features.title: Enterprise-grade compliance
features.description: Supports regulatory requirements across industries with certifications and customer-managed encryption via Box KeySafe.

pros: Established, publicly traded company with over two decades of enterprise track record
Deep integrations with major productivity suites like Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace
Strong compliance and governance features suited to regulated industries
Growing suite of AI-powered content features under active investment
cons: Higher-tier plans and add-ons such as Shield, Governance, and KeySafe increase total cost significantly
Enterprise and Enterprise Plus pricing is not transparent and requires a sales conversation
Three-user minimum on business plans is not ideal for solo users or very small teams
Feature-rich platform can require onboarding and admin configuration to use effectively

faqs.question: Is Box publicly traded?
faqs.answer: Yes, Box, Inc. trades on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker symbol BOX and has been public since its January 2015 IPO.

faqs.question: How much does Box cost?
faqs.answer: Box's published business plans start at 5 dollars per user per month for Business Starter and range up to 35 dollars per user per month for Enterprise, all billed annually with a three-user minimum; Enterprise Plus is custom priced.

faqs.question: Who founded Box?
faqs.answer: Box was founded in 2005 by Aaron Levie and Dylan Smith, with Levie continuing to serve as CEO.

faqs.question: How much revenue does Box generate?
faqs.answer: Box reported approximately 1.18 billion dollars in revenue for fiscal year 2026, up roughly 8 percent year over year.

faqs.question: How many employees does Box have?
faqs.answer: Box had approximately 2,912 employees as of the end of fiscal year 2026.

faqs.question: What is Box AI?
faqs.answer: Box AI is Box's suite of AI-powered features for document summarization, question answering, data extraction, and classification across stored content.

faqs.question: Does Box have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Box offers a free Individual plan with limited storage intended for personal use.

faqs.question: What integrations does Box support?
faqs.answer: Box integrates with major productivity platforms including Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, Salesforce, and Slack.

use_cases.title: Enterprise document collaboration
use_cases.description: A large enterprise uses Box as a secure central repository for cross-team document collaboration with granular permission controls.

use_cases.title: Regulated industry compliance
use_cases.description: A healthcare or financial services organization uses Box Governance and Shield to meet retention and security compliance requirements.
use_cases.audience: Compliance and legal teams in regulated industries

use_cases.title: AI-assisted content workflows
use_cases.description: A business uses Box AI to automatically summarize contracts and extract key data points from large volumes of stored documents.
use_cases.audience: Legal, finance, and operations teams handling document-heavy workflows

features: Expanded storage and file size limits
Larger file upload limits up to 15 GB
Highest tier of security and support
